text: This is an instructional video demonstrating a right robotic XI nephroureterectomy with bladder cuff excision performed at Thomas Jefferson University Hospital in
Our patient is a seventy four year old female who presented with a single episode of gross hematuria with clots.
She has no history of stones or GU malignancy.
She did have a prior twenty pack year smoking history and no additional toxin exposure.
She had a negative urine culture, her serum creatinine was one point zero.

Her CT urogram demonstrated a right kidney with a three point one times two point six times three point zero centimeter enhancing mass that filled the entire right collecting system with mild hydronephrosis.
The patient subsequently underwent cystoscopy, ureteroscopy with biopsy confirming a high grade upper tract urothelial carcinoma without any evidence of disease in the bladder.
Based on staging studies, she had clinical T-N-M-U-T-U-C.
She was counseled on the need for radical nephro-ureterectomy and the possible role for neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
She opted to undergo dose-dense MVAC neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
Following NAC, she was scheduled for robotic right radical NU regional lymphadenectomy and bladder cuff excision approximately six weeks later.
The patient is positioned in the left lateral decubitus position, lying on her left side to facilitate access to the surgical site for the nephrectomy procedure.
The XI port placement is noted in the slide below.
The use of the SI robot necessitates different port placement, often requiring the robot to be redocked.
Hence, the use of XI robot is ideal for nephro-uriderectomy.
A grasper is inserted through a five-millimeter port to function as a liver retractor, elevating the liver to improve visibility of the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the white line of Toldt, facilitating the medial reflection of the ascending colon from the hepatic flexure down to the curve of the cecum.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum and performs a Kocher maneuver, mobilizing it to expose the underlying structures.
The surgical field reveals the inferior vena cava and renal vein, clearly visible within the operative area.
The surgeon follows the inferior vena cava (IVC) inferiorly, continuing dissection laterally to the IVC until the psoas muscle is visible beneath the tail of Girauda's fascia.
The gonadal vein and ureter are visible, coursing over the psoas muscle.
The surgeon circumferentially undermines the ureter and secures it with two Hem-o-lok clips for oncologic control, targeting tumors of the renal pelvis or upper ureter.
The surgeon continues the renal dissection, focusing on separating the kidney from surrounding tissues and structures.
The surgeon places the kidney under tension and continues dissection superiorly along the inferior vena cava, revealing the renal hilum.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic stapler to ligate the renal artery and vein after they have been dissected free from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic stapler to staple the renal artery, ensuring secure ligation before proceeding with other structures.
The surgeon uses a ligature to mobilize the superior aspect of the kidney, carefully avoiding the adrenal gland during the dissection.
The surgeon leaves the lateral and superior attachments of the kidney intact to provide traction while dissecting the ureter, ensuring stability during the procedure.
The surgeon performs a regional lymphadenectomy, focusing on the paracaval and intraaorticaval areas, in the context of a right renal pelvic UTUC.
The surgeon places the ureter under tension and dissects it along its length towards the bladder, with the dissection reaching the area marked by the obliterated umbilical artery.
The surgeon mobilizes the bladder slightly anteriorly, providing clear exposure for the subsequent closure procedure.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to make an incision into the bladder superior to the ureteral orifice, creating an entry point for further dissection.
The surgeon identifies the ureteral orifice and performs circumferential dissection around it, ensuring the creation of a small bladder cuff.
The surgeon places a V-lock absorbable suture at the inferior apex of the bladder cuff to maintain tension and facilitate closure before the cuff is completely excised.
The surgeon excises the remaining bladder cuff, completing the circumferential dissection around the ureteral orifice.
The surgeon closes the bladder cuff using full-thickness V-lock absorbable sutures, stitching bidirectionally and tying the sutures in the middle.
The surgeon fills the bladder with 120 to 180 cc of sterile water or saline, visually checking for leaks at the bladder cuff.
The surgeon sutures closed the peritoneal reflection, completing the closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon dissects and frees the remaining lateral attachments of the kidney, separating it from surrounding tissues.
